questions.question:
Identify the ONE CORRECT matching between the OSI layers and their corresponding functionalities as shown.

OSI Layers:
(a) Network Layer
(b) Transport Layer
(c) Datalink layer

Functionalities:
(I) Packet Routing
(II) Framing and error handling
(III) Host to host communication

A. (a)-(I), (b)-(II), (c)-(III)
B. (a)-(I), (b)-(III), (c)-(II)
C. (a)-(II), (b)-(III), (c)-(I)
D. (a)-(III), (b)-(I), (c)-(II)
questions.to_find: The correct matching of OSI layers to their functionalities

understand.plain: The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model divides network communication into 7 layers, each with a distinct role. This question tests whether you know which layer handles packet routing, framing/error handling, and host-to-host communication. Each layer has a specific job and we need to map each layer to the right job.

keywords.explain: Responsible for logical addressing and routing of packets from source to destination across multiple networks.
keywords.example: IP protocol operates at the Network layer and uses IP addresses for routing.

keywords.explain: Provides end-to-end (host-to-host) communication, flow control, and reliability between processes on different hosts.
keywords.example: TCP and UDP operate at the Transport layer; TCP ensures reliable delivery.

keywords.explain: Handles framing, physical addressing (MAC), and error detection/correction for data transmitted over a single link.
keywords.example: Ethernet uses the Data Link layer to add MAC addresses and CRC for error detection.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="520" height="220" viewBox="0 0 520 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="220" height="50" class="hi"/><text x="120" y="42" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">Network Layer (3)</text><rect x="10" y="70" width="220" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="120" y="102" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">Transport Layer (4)</text><rect x="10" y="130" width="220" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="120" y="162" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">Data Link Layer (2)</text><line x1="230" y1="35" x2="290" y2="35" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/><line x1="230" y1="95" x2="290" y2="155" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/><line x1="230" y1="155" x2="290" y2="95" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/><rect x="290" y="10" width="220" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="400" y="42" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">(I) Packet Routing</text><rect x="290" y="70" width="220" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="400" y="95" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">(II) Framing & error</text><text x="400" y="113" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">handling</text><rect x="290" y="130" width="220" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="400" y="155" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">(III) Host to host</text><text x="400" y="173" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">communication</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three OSI layers on the left (Network, Transport, Data Link) connected by arrows to three functionalities on the right (Packet Routing, Framing and error handling, Host to host communication). Network Layer maps to Packet Routing, Transport Layer maps to Host to host communication, Data Link maps to Framing and error handling.

given.aim: Match each OSI layer to its primary functionality

terms.meaning: Layer 3 of OSI; handles logical addressing and inter-network routing
terms.connects: Its main job is Packet Routing (I)

terms.meaning: Layer 4 of OSI; provides end-to-end reliable or unreliable delivery between hosts
terms.connects: Its main job is Host-to-host communication (III)

terms.meaning: Layer 2 of OSI; organizes bits into frames and detects/corrects errors on a single link
terms.connects: Its main job is Framing and error handling (II)

given.plan: Recall each OSI layer's primary function: Network layer routes packets between networks, Transport layer provides host-to-host (end-to-end) communication, and Data Link layer handles framing and error detection within a single link. Match accordingly.

steps.title: Match Network Layer
steps.apply: The Network Layer (Layer 3) is responsible for routing packets across multiple networks using logical addresses (e.g., IP). Therefore (a) Network Layer maps to (I) Packet Routing.
steps.note: Routers operate at the Network Layer and make routing decisions.

steps.title: Match Transport Layer
steps.apply: The Transport Layer (Layer 4) provides end-to-end (host-to-host) communication between processes running on different hosts. Therefore (b) Transport Layer maps to (III) Host to host communication.
steps.note: TCP/UDP operate at this layer. 'End-to-end' and 'host-to-host' are synonymous in OSI context.

steps.title: Match Data Link Layer
steps.apply: The Data Link Layer (Layer 2) packages raw bits into frames, adds MAC addresses, and performs error detection/correction (e.g., CRC). Therefore (c) Datalink layer maps to (II) Framing and error handling.
steps.note: This narrows the answer to option B.

solution.result: The correct matching is (a)-(I), (b)-(III), (c)-(II), which is option B.

questions.formula_note: OSI layer responsibilities: Network (Layer 3) = Routing, Transport (Layer 4) = End-to-end/host-to-host delivery, Data Link (Layer 2) = Framing and error handling.

questions.question: Suppose a 5-bit message is transmitted from a source to a destination through a noisy channel. The probability that a bit of the message gets flipped during transmission is 0.1. Flipping of each bit is independent of one another. The probability that the message is delivered error-free to the destination is ________ (rounded off to three decimal places).
questions.to_find: Probability that all 5 bits are transmitted without any flip (error-free delivery)

understand.plain: A 5-bit message is sent over a noisy channel where each bit independently has a 10% chance of being flipped (corrupted). We need to find the probability that none of the 5 bits get flipped, meaning the message arrives perfectly intact. Since bit flips are independent, we multiply the probabilities.

keywords.explain: The probability that a single bit changes from 0 to 1 or 1 to 0 during transmission due to noise.
keywords.example: If p=0.1, a bit sent as '1' has 10% chance of arriving as '0'.

keywords.explain: Two events are independent if the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other.
keywords.example: Each bit flip is independent, so P(bit1 ok AND bit2 ok) = P(bit1 ok) * P(bit2 ok).

keywords.explain: All bits in the message arrive at the destination exactly as sent, with no flips.
keywords.example: For a 5-bit message, all 5 bits must be received correctly.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="460" height="130" viewBox="0 0 460 130" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="40" width="60" height="50" class="hi"/><text x="40" y="72" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">b1</text><rect x="80" y="40" width="60" height="50" class="hi"/><text x="110" y="72" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">b2</text><rect x="150" y="40" width="60" height="50" class="hi"/><text x="180" y="72" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">b3</text><rect x="220" y="40" width="60" height="50" class="hi"/><text x="250" y="72" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">b4</text><rect x="290" y="40" width="60" height="50" class="hi"/><text x="320" y="72" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">b5</text><text x="230" y="20" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">5-bit message: each bit ok with P=0.9</text><text x="230" y="115" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">P(error-free) = 0.9^5 = 0.59049 ≈ 0.590</text><text x="360" y="72" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">x 0.9</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Five boxes labeled b1 to b5 representing 5 bits, each transmitted successfully with probability 0.9. The total probability of error-free delivery is 0.9 raised to the power 5.

given.aim: Find the probability that a 5-bit message is transmitted without any bit errors

terms.meaning: Number of bits in the message
terms.connects: We need all 5 bits to arrive correctly

terms.meaning: Probability that any single bit is flipped during transmission
terms.example: 1 in 10 bits gets corrupted on average
terms.connects: Probability a single bit is NOT flipped = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9

terms.meaning: The event that bit i is flipped is independent of whether bit j is flipped
terms.example: Whether bit 1 flips has no effect on bit 2 flipping
terms.connects: Allows us to multiply individual probabilities: P(all ok) = 0.9^5

given.plan: Since each bit's success (no flip) has probability 0.9 and the events are independent, the probability that all 5 bits arrive correctly is the product of 5 independent probabilities: 0.9^5. Compute and round to 3 decimal places.

steps.title: Probability a single bit is correct
steps.apply: Given p = 0.1 is the probability of a bit being flipped, the probability that a given bit is NOT flipped (arrives correctly) is 1 - 0.1 = 0.9.
steps.note: This is the complement of the bit-flip event.

steps.title: Probability all 5 bits are correct
steps.apply:
Since all 5 bits must be correct and flips are independent:
P(error-free) = 0.9 * 0.9 * 0.9 * 0.9 * 0.9 = 0.9^5
= 0.59049
steps.note: This uses the multiplication rule for independent events.

steps.title: Round to 3 decimal places
steps.apply: 0.59049 rounded to three decimal places is 0.590 (the fourth decimal is 4, which rounds down).

solution.result: The probability that the message is delivered error-free is 0.590.

questions.formula_note: P(error-free) = (1-p)^n where p is bit flip probability and n is message length. Uses independence of bit errors and the multiplication rule for independent events.

questions.question:
In a network of LANs connected by bridges, packets are sent from one LAN to another through intermediate bridges. Since more than one path may exist between two LANs, packets may have to be routed through multiple bridges. Why is the spanning tree algorithm used for bridge-routing?

A. For shortest path routing between LANs
B. For avoiding loops in the routing paths
C. For fault tolerance
D. For minimizing collisions
questions.answer: B. For avoiding loops in the routing paths
questions.to_find: The reason spanning tree algorithm is used for bridge-routing in a LAN network

understand.plain: When multiple LANs are connected by bridges (Layer 2 devices), there may be multiple physical paths between any two LANs. If there are loops in the network topology, packets can circulate indefinitely (broadcast storms), consuming all available bandwidth. The spanning tree algorithm creates a loop-free logical topology so packets have exactly one path between any two nodes.

keywords.explain: A Layer 2 network device that connects two or more LAN segments and forwards frames based on MAC addresses.
keywords.example: A bridge between LAN-A and LAN-B forwards packets with destination MAC on LAN-B.

keywords.explain: An algorithm that finds a spanning tree of the network graph — a tree that connects all nodes with no cycles, eliminating loops while maintaining connectivity.
keywords.example: If LANs A, B, C form a triangle via bridges, STP disables one bridge port to break the loop.

keywords.explain: When a broadcast packet loops indefinitely through a network with cycles, consuming all bandwidth and causing network failure.
keywords.example: Without STP, a broadcast on LAN-A would circulate forever through the loop.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="400" height="220" viewBox="0 0 400 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><ellipse cx="200" cy="40" rx="50" ry="25" class="bg"/><text x="200" y="47" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">LAN A</text><ellipse cx="80" cy="170" rx="50" ry="25" class="bg"/><text x="80" y="177" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">LAN B</text><ellipse cx="320" cy="170" rx="50" ry="25" class="bg"/><text x="320" y="177" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">LAN C</text><line x1="155" y1="58" x2="110" y2="148" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="3"/><text x="110" y="105" class="tag ink">B1</text><line x1="245" y1="58" x2="280" y2="148" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="3"/><text x="272" y="105" class="tag ink">B2</text><line x1="130" y1="170" x2="270" y2="170" stroke="#D85A30" stroke-width="3" stroke-dasharray="8,4"/><text x="200" y="165" class="tag no" text-anchor="middle">B3 (disabled by STP)</text><text x="200" y="210" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">STP disables B3 to remove the loop</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three LANs (A, B, C) connected in a triangle by bridges B1, B2, and B3. STP disables bridge B3 (shown as dashed red line) to break the loop, leaving a spanning tree with bridges B1 and B2 active.

given.aim: Determine why the spanning tree algorithm is used for routing in a bridged LAN network

terms.meaning: Multiple LAN segments interconnected by Layer 2 bridge devices
terms.example: LAN1 -- Bridge1 -- LAN2 -- Bridge2 -- LAN3 -- Bridge3 -- LAN1 (loop)
terms.connects: Multiple paths between LANs can form cycles

terms.meaning: More than one route exists from source LAN to destination LAN due to redundant bridges
terms.example: Can go A->B->C or A->C directly
terms.connects: Cycles in the graph cause forwarding loops

terms.meaning: A subgraph of the network that connects all LANs with no cycles, using a subset of available bridge links
terms.example: From a 3-node cycle, remove one edge to get a spanning tree with 2 edges
terms.connects: Guarantees a unique, loop-free path between any two LANs

given.plan: Analyze why loops in a bridged LAN are problematic (broadcast storms, duplicate packets) and how spanning tree algorithm addresses this. STA is NOT about shortest path, fault tolerance, or collision avoidance — it is specifically about eliminating loops.

steps.title: Identify the problem with loops in bridged LANs
steps.apply: Bridges forward packets based on MAC addresses and forward broadcast frames to all ports. If loops exist in the bridge topology, a broadcast packet will be forwarded in a cycle indefinitely (broadcast storm). This saturates the network and causes duplicate packet delivery, crashing the network.
steps.note: Unlike routers which have TTL to discard looping packets, bridges at Layer 2 have no such mechanism.

steps.title: Role of spanning tree algorithm
steps.apply: The spanning tree algorithm (IEEE 802.1D STP) selects a subset of bridge links to form a tree that connects all LANs without any cycles. Bridge ports that would form a loop are placed in 'blocking' state. This ensures exactly one active path between any two LANs, preventing forwarding loops.
steps.note: The algorithm does NOT optimize for shortest path — that is done at Layer 3 (routing protocols).

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: A: Shortest path — STP does not guarantee shortest path, it blocks some links. C: Fault tolerance — STP actually reduces redundancy (blocking backup paths). D: Minimizing collisions — collision domains are a Layer 1/CSMA concept, not what STP addresses. Only B (avoiding loops) is correct.
steps.note: When STP is active, redundant paths are blocked (not used), so fault tolerance is actually reduced, not improved.

solution.result: The spanning tree algorithm is used for bridge-routing to avoid loops in the routing paths (option B).

questions.formula_note: IEEE 802.1D Spanning Tree Protocol (STP): computes a loop-free spanning tree over the bridged LAN topology by electing a root bridge and blocking redundant ports, preventing broadcast storms.

questions.question:
Consider the following three statements about link state and distance vector routing protocols, for a large network with N network nodes and M links.

[S1]: The computational overhead in link state protocols is higher than in distance vector protocols.
[S2]: A distance vector protocol (with split horizon) avoids persistent routing loops.
[S3]: After a topology change, a link state protocol will converge faster than a distance vector protocol.

Which one of the following is correct about S1, S2, and S3?

A. S1, S2, and S3 are all true.
B. S1, S2, and S3 are all false.
C. S1 and S2 are true, but S3 is false.
D. S1 and S3 are true, but S2 is false.
questions.answer: D. S1 and S3 are true, but S2 is false.
questions.to_find: Which combination of S1, S2, S3 is correct about link state vs. distance vector routing protocols

understand.plain: This question compares two major types of routing protocols: Link State (like OSPF) and Distance Vector (like RIP). We need to evaluate three statements about their computational overhead, loop avoidance, and convergence speed. Understanding the properties of each protocol type is key to answering this.

keywords.explain: Each router floods its local link state information to all routers; every router then has a complete map of the network and runs Dijkstra's algorithm to compute shortest paths.
keywords.example: OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) is a link state protocol.

keywords.explain: Each router only knows distances to destinations and which neighbor to use; routers exchange their routing tables with neighbors periodically.
keywords.example: RIP (Routing Information Protocol) is a distance vector protocol.

keywords.explain: A technique in distance vector routing where a router does not advertise a route back to the neighbor from which it learned that route, reducing count-to-infinity problems.
keywords.example: If R1 learned path to X via R2, R1 does not advertise X back to R2.

keywords.explain: In distance vector protocols, when a link fails, routers may keep increasing their distance estimate in a loop. Split horizon reduces but does not fully prevent all such loops.
keywords.example: In a 3-router cycle, split horizon alone cannot prevent count-to-infinity.

understand.visual_alt: Comparison table: Link State protocol uses Dijkstra (O(N^2) or O(M log N)) so higher computational overhead but fast convergence. Distance Vector uses Bellman-Ford distributed, lower per-node compute but slower convergence (count-to-infinity problem). Split horizon reduces but does not eliminate persistent loops in all topologies.

given.aim: Evaluate three statements about link state vs. distance vector routing protocol properties

terms.meaning: Size of the network — N routers and M bidirectional links connecting them
terms.example: A network with 100 routers and 200 links
terms.connects: Computational complexity scales with N and M for Dijkstra's algorithm

terms.meaning: Link state requires Dijkstra's algorithm O(N^2) or O(M log N) at each router, plus flooding overhead O(N*M)
terms.example: With 100 nodes, each router runs Dijkstra on the full graph
terms.connects: Higher computation than distance vector which only processes neighbor updates

terms.term: S2: Split horizon and persistent loops
terms.meaning: Split horizon prevents simple two-node loops but cannot prevent loops in topologies with 3 or more nodes
terms.example: In a triangle R1-R2-R3, if R1-R3 link fails, split horizon cannot stop a routing loop between all three
terms.connects: Split horizon is insufficient to completely prevent all persistent routing loops

terms.meaning: Link state protocols converge faster because each router has the full network map and immediately recalculates; distance vector must propagate updates hop-by-hop
terms.example: OSPF converges in seconds; RIP may take minutes due to count-to-infinity
terms.connects: Link state converges faster than distance vector after topology changes

given.plan: Evaluate each statement independently: S1 (link state has higher computation — TRUE due to Dijkstra on global topology), S2 (split horizon prevents ALL persistent loops — FALSE, only prevents 2-node loops), S3 (link state converges faster — TRUE). Then find the matching option.

steps.title: Evaluate S1: Computational overhead
steps.formula_raw: LS: O(N^2) Dijkstra per node + O(N*M) flooding; DV: O(N) per update iteration
steps.apply: In link state protocols, each router maintains the full network topology and runs Dijkstra's algorithm (O(N^2) with naive implementation, O(M log N) with priority queue). Additionally, link state advertisements (LSAs) are flooded throughout the entire network. In distance vector, each router only runs a simplified Bellman-Ford considering its immediate neighbors. Therefore, S1 is TRUE: link state has higher computational overhead.
steps.note: The flooding mechanism in link state also adds significant bandwidth and processing overhead for large N.

steps.title: Evaluate S2: Split horizon and persistent loops
steps.apply: Split horizon prevents a router from advertising a route back to the neighbor it learned that route from. This prevents simple two-node routing loops (A tells B about X, B tells A back). However, in networks with three or more nodes forming a cycle, split horizon alone CANNOT prevent all persistent routing loops (the count-to-infinity problem can still occur in triangle or larger topologies). Therefore, S2 is FALSE.
steps.note: To fully prevent loops, 'split horizon with poisoned reverse' or 'path vector' (like BGP) is needed, but even these have limitations in complex topologies.

steps.title: Evaluate S3: Convergence speed after topology change
steps.apply: In link state protocols, when a link changes, the affected router floods the update across the entire network. All routers receive the update and immediately recompute their routing tables using Dijkstra. In distance vector protocols, routing updates propagate gradually hop-by-hop (Bellman-Ford), which can take many update cycles to converge, especially with count-to-infinity. Therefore, S3 is TRUE: link state converges faster.
steps.note: OSPF (link state) typically converges in seconds; RIP (distance vector) can take minutes.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: S1 = TRUE, S2 = FALSE, S3 = TRUE. This matches option D: S1 and S3 are true, but S2 is false.
steps.note: Option A is eliminated because S2 is false. Options B and C are also eliminated.

solution.result: S1 and S3 are true, but S2 is false — option D.

questions.formula_note: Link state uses Dijkstra's algorithm (high compute, fast convergence); Distance vector uses distributed Bellman-Ford (low compute per node, slow convergence, split horizon does not prevent all persistent loops).
